questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If projected with identical velocity perpendicular to a magnetic field, which of the following particles will be subjected to a magnetic force of maximum magnitude?
questions.solution:
The magnetic force on a charged particle moving perpendicular to a magnetic field is given by $F = qvB$.

Since the velocity $v$ and magnetic field $B$ are identical for all particles, the magnitude of the magnetic force is directly proportional to the magnitude of the charge $q$ of the particle.

The magnitudes of the charges for the given particles are:
Proton: $e$
$\text{He}^+$: $e$
Electron: $e$
$\text{Li}^{++}$: $2e$

Since $\text{Li}^{++}$ has the maximum charge magnitude, it will experience the maximum magnetic force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A positively charged particle projected towards the east is deflected northwards by a magnetic field. The magnetic field could be directed
questions.solution:
The magnetic force on a moving charge is given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

Given that the particle is positively charged ($q > 0$), the direction of the force is the same as the direction of $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$.

Let the east direction be along the positive $x$-axis ($\hat{i}$), the north direction be along the positive $y$-axis ($\hat{j}$), and the upward direction be along the positive $z$-axis ($\hat{k}$).

The velocity of the particle is $\vec{v} = v\hat{i}$.

The magnetic force is $\vec{F} = F\hat{j}$.

Let the magnetic field be $\vec{B} = B_x\hat{i} + B_y\hat{j} + B_z\hat{k}$.

Substituting these into the force equation:

$F\hat{j} = q(v\hat{i} \times (B_x\hat{i} + B_y\hat{j} + B_z\hat{k}))$

$F\hat{j} = qv(B_y\hat{k} - B_z\hat{j})$

For the force to be directed towards the north ($\hat{j}$ direction), the $\hat{k}$ component must be zero and the $\hat{j}$ component must be positive.

This gives $B_y = 0$ and $-qvB_z > 0$.

Since $q$ and $v$ are positive, we must have $B_z < 0$.

A negative $z$-component indicates that the magnetic field must have a component directed downward. Thus, the magnetic field could be directed downward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following particles will exhibit the minimum frequency of revolution when projected with identical velocities perpendicular to a magnetic field?
questions.solution:
The frequency of revolution of a charged particle in a uniform magnetic field is given by $f = \dfrac{qB}{2\pi m}$.

This shows that the frequency is independent of the velocity of the particle and is directly proportional to its specific charge $\dfrac{q}{m}$.

Let us compare the specific charge $\dfrac{q}{m}$ for the given particles:

For an electron, $\dfrac{q}{m} = \dfrac{e}{m_e}$

For a proton, $\dfrac{q}{m} = \dfrac{e}{m_p}$

For $\text{He}^+$, $\dfrac{q}{m} = \dfrac{e}{4m_p}$

For $\text{Li}^+$, $\dfrac{q}{m} = \dfrac{e}{7m_p}$

Since $\text{Li}^+$ has the largest mass among the given ions with the same magnitude of charge $e$, it has the minimum value of $\dfrac{q}{m}$.

Therefore, $\text{Li}^+$ will exhibit the minimum frequency of revolution.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle, attached to a string fixed at one point, is whirled in a horizontal circle on a frictionless table. If a magnetic field is switched on in the vertical direction, the tension in the string
questions.solution:
Initially, the tension in the string provides the necessary centripetal force for the circular motion of the charged particle. Let the mass of the particle be $m$, its speed be $v$, and the radius of the circle be $r$. The initial tension is given by:

$T_{0} = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$

When a vertical magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is switched on, a magnetic Lorentz force $\vec{F}_{m} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ acts on the particle. Since the velocity $\vec{v}$ is in the horizontal plane and $\vec{B}$ is vertical, the magnetic force will act along the radial direction (either towards the center or away from the center).

Depending on the sign of the charge $q$, the direction of the velocity $\vec{v}$, and the direction of the magnetic field $\vec{B}$, the magnetic force $F_{m} = qvB$ can be directed radially inward or radially outward.

If $\vec{F}_{m}$ is directed radially inward, the new tension $T$ is:

$T + F_{m} = \dfrac{mv^2}{r} \Rightarrow T = \dfrac{mv^2}{r} - F_{m}$ (Tension decreases)

If $\vec{F}_{m}$ is directed radially outward, the new tension $T$ is:

$T - F_{m} = \dfrac{mv^2}{r} \Rightarrow T = \dfrac{mv^2}{r} + F_{m}$ (Tension increases)

Since the specific directions and the sign of the charge are not provided, the tension in the string may increase or decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following particles will trace out the smallest circle if projected with identical velocity perpendicular to a magnetic field?
questions.solution:
The radius of the circular path of a charged particle moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field is given by $r = \dfrac{mv}{qB}$.

Since the velocity $v$ and the magnetic field $B$ are identical for all the given particles, the radius $r$ is directly proportional to the mass-to-charge ratio $\dfrac{m}{q}$.

For the given particles, the magnitude of the charge $q$ is the same (equal to $e$). Thus, the radius is directly proportional to the mass of the particle ($r \propto m$).

Among $\text{He}^+$, $\text{Li}^+$, electron, and proton, the electron has the smallest mass ($m_e \approx \dfrac{m_p}{1836}$).

Therefore, the electron will trace out the smallest circle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular loop with an area of $1 \text{ cm}^2$ carrying a $10 \text{ A}$ current is positioned in a $0.1 \text{ T}$ magnetic field that is perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The torque exerted on the loop by the magnetic field is
questions.solution:
The magnetic moment of the circular loop is given by $M = IA$.

Since the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop, the angle $\theta$ between the area vector of the loop and the magnetic field is $0^{\circ}$.

The torque exerted on the loop is given by $\tau = MB \sin \theta$.

Substituting $\theta = 0^{\circ}$, we get $\tau = MB \sin 0^{\circ} = 0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A beam comprising protons and electrons, travelling at the same speed, traverses a thin region containing a magnetic field perpendicular to the beam. The protons and the electrons
options: will deviate by unequal angles and thereby separate
will deviate by an equal angle but will separate
will deviate by an equal angle and remain together
questions.solution:
The radius of the circular path of a charged particle in a perpendicular magnetic field is given by $R = \dfrac{mv}{qB}$.

For a thin region of magnetic field of width $d$, the angle of deviation $\theta$ is given by $\sin\theta = \dfrac{d}{R} = \dfrac{qBd}{mv}$.

Since protons and electrons have different masses ($m_p \gg m_e$) but the same magnitude of charge and same speed, their radii of curvature will be different ($R_p > R_e$).

Therefore, their angles of deviation will be unequal ($\theta_p < \theta_e$).

Additionally, due to their opposite charges, they will experience magnetic forces in opposite directions. Thus, they will deviate by unequal angles and thereby separate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform circular wire of radius $a$ has an electric current $i$ entering and exiting through diametrically opposite points. Passing through the centre at a speed $v$, a charged particle $q$ travels along the axis of the circular wire. What is the magnitude of the magnetic force acting on the particle at the instant it crosses the centre?
questions.solution:
When the current $i$ enters the uniform circular wire at one point and exits at the diametrically opposite point, it divides equally into two semicircular paths. The current in each semicircular part is $\dfrac{i}{2}$.

The magnetic field at the centre due to the first semicircular part is $B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 (i/2)}{4a}$, directed perpendicular to the plane of the loop (say, inwards).

The magnetic field at the centre due to the second semicircular part is $B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 (i/2)}{4a}$, directed perpendicular to the plane of the loop in the opposite direction (outwards).

The net magnetic field at the centre of the circular wire is the vector sum of these two fields:

$B_{net} = B_1 - B_2 = 0$

The magnetic force on a charged particle moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ is given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

Since the net magnetic field at the centre is zero, the magnetic force acting on the particle at the instant it crosses the centre is also zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle travels in a region containing a uniform magnetic field and a parallel, uniform electric field. At a specific instant, the particle's velocity is perpendicular to the direction of the fields. The path taken by the particle will be
questions.solution:
Let the uniform electric and magnetic fields be directed along the z-axis, so $\vec{E} = E \hat{k}$ and $\vec{B} = B \hat{k}$.

The initial velocity of the particle is perpendicular to the fields. Let $\vec{v} = v_0 \hat{i}$.

The magnetic force acting on the particle is $\vec{F}_m = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$, which provides the necessary centripetal force to move the particle in a circular path in the plane perpendicular to the magnetic field. The radius of this circular path is constant since the perpendicular speed remains constant.

The electric force acting on the particle is $\vec{F}_e = q\vec{E}$, which provides a constant acceleration $a = \dfrac{qE}{m}$ along the direction of the fields.

Due to this acceleration, the component of velocity parallel to the fields increases continuously with time ($v_{\parallel} = at$).

The combination of uniform circular motion in the perpendicular plane and accelerated motion along the parallel direction results in a helical path.

The pitch of the helix is the distance traveled along the magnetic field during one complete revolution. Since the velocity along the field is increasing, the pitch increases with time. Thus, the path is a helix with nonuniform pitch.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle is moving through a uniform magnetic field. At a given instant, the particle's velocity forms an acute angle with the magnetic field. The path followed by the particle will be
questions.solution:
Let the magnetic field be $\vec{B}$ and the velocity of the charged particle be $\vec{v}$.

The velocity $\vec{v}$ can be resolved into two components: $v_{\parallel} = v \cos \theta$ parallel to the magnetic field and $v_{\perp} = v \sin \theta$ perpendicular to the magnetic field, where $\theta$ is the acute angle between $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$.

The perpendicular component $v_{\perp}$ provides the necessary centripetal force, causing the particle to move in a circular path of radius $r = \dfrac{mv_{\perp}}{qB}$.

The parallel component $v_{\parallel}$ remains constant since there is no force acting along the direction of the magnetic field.

The combination of uniform circular motion and uniform linear motion results in a helical path.

The pitch of the helix is the distance traveled along the magnetic field in one time period $T$, given by $p = v_{\parallel} T = v \cos \theta \left(\dfrac{2\pi m}{qB}\right)$.

Since $v$, $\theta$, $m$, $q$, and $B$ are all constant, the pitch of the helix is uniform.
